summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's surprise settlement with Qualcomm, arguing it was driven by Intel's failure to deliver viable 5G modems on schedule. He dismisses claims that a 5G iPhone could ship in 2019, noting Apple's multi-year development pipeline and the immaturity of 5G networks. The settlement was bitter but necessary because Apple faced a future without 5G devices if they continued relying on Intel, whose efforts were falling far behind. Gruber draws a parallel to the 4G/LTE transition, where Apple was similarly 'late' but shipped a superior product. He predicts 2020 as the realistic first year for 5G iPhones, arguing Apple's goal should be shipping one of the first good 5G phones rather than simply one of the first.
key_points: Intel's 5G modem development was so far behind schedule that Apple couldn't count on them even for 2020 iPhones, forcing the Qualcomm settlement
Apple has 1,000-1,200 engineers working on in-house modem chips, but those are years away from production readiness
The 4G/LTE timeline is instructive: Android shipped LTE phones in 2011, Apple waited until the iPhone 5 in 2012, and the delay didn't hurt them
Current 5G technology is too immature for Apple's standards — networks aren't ready and chips aren't efficient enough
The settlement highlights the strategic danger of relying on third parties for critical components like cellular modems
money_quotes: Settling with Qualcomm was bitter medicine, but not having 5G devices until 2021 would be untenable.
Apple's goal shouldn't be to ship one of the first 5G phones — it should be to ship one of the first good 5G phones.
Apple doesn't make boutique iPhones — they're not going to do a 5G iPhone that is intended to sell in limited quantities just to say they have a 5G phone.

Apple's legal/licensing squabble with Qualcomm and Intel's technical failure shows that these chips are too important for Apple to rely on a third party — especially now that Qualcomm effectively has a monopoly on 5G chips.
Just for kicks, imagine where Apple would be if they had never succeeded in developing their own in-house CPUs and systems-on-a-chip. Qualcomm would have them over a barrel.
When new iPhones ship without 5G networking this fall, there will be headlines galore suggesting that Apple is late to the 5G game. But that was also the case in 2011 with LTE.
key_insight: Apple settled with Qualcomm not out of weakness but out of strategic necessity — Intel's 5G failure left them no viable path to shipping 5G iPhones by 2020, and the settlement buys time while Apple builds its own in-house modem capability.
